title: Splitting Element IDs for Parallel Processing
text: ModelArray supports within-process parallelism via n_cores parameter - multiple CPU cores single R session working elements parallel. many datasets, sufficient. However, large datasets (e.g., 600,000+ fixels thousands subjects), even multi-core single job may take long. cases, can split elements across multiple independent HPC jobs, processing different range elements.

title: Splitting Element IDs for Parallel Processing
text: Use numElementsTotal() get total element count, divide ranges:
code: library(ModelArray)  modelarray <- ModelArray("data.h5", scalar_types = c("FDC")) n_total <- numElementsTotal(modelarray, "FDC")  # Split into chunks of 100,000 elements chunk_size <- 100000 starts <- seq(1, n_total, by = chunk_size) ends <- pmin(starts + chunk_size - 1, n_total)  # Each job gets one of these ranges for (i in seq_along(starts)) {   cat(sprintf("Job %d: element.subset = %d:%d\n", i, starts[i], ends[i])) } Job 1: element.subset = 1:100000 Job 2: element.subset = 100001:200000 Job 3: element.subset = 200001:300000 Job 4: element.subset = 300001:400000 Job 5: element.subset = 400001:500000 Job 6: element.subset = 500001:600000 Job 7: element.subset = 600001:602229

title: Splitting Element IDs for Parallel Processing
text: job runs model assigned range elements. example, R script parameterized job index: HPC cluster job array (e.g., SLURM):
code: # run_chunk.R # Usage: Rscript run_chunk.R <job_index>  args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE) job_index <- as.integer(args[1])  library(ModelArray)  modelarray <- ModelArray("data.h5", scalar_types = c("FDC")) phenotypes <- read.csv("cohort.csv")  chunk_size <- 100000 n_total <- numElementsTotal(modelarray, "FDC") start <- (job_index - 1) * chunk_size + 1 end <- min(job_index * chunk_size, n_total)  result <- ModelArray.lm(   FDC ~ Age + sex + motion,   modelarray, phenotypes, "FDC",   element.subset = start:end,   n_cores = 4 )  # Save this chunk to its own HDF5 file # (safe because each job writes to a different file) chunk_h5 <- sprintf("result_chunk_%d.h5", job_index) writeResults(chunk_h5, df.output = result, analysis_name = "results_lm_chunk") #!/bin/bash #SBATCH --array=1-7 #SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4  Rscript run_chunk.R $SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID

what: Combining results and writing to HDF5
title: Splitting Element IDs for Parallel Processing
text: jobs complete, read chunk .h5, concatenate rows, write one final result data.h5: Important: one process write HDF5 file time. HDF5 support concurrent writes multiple processes — see vignette("hdf5-large-analyses") details .
code: library(ModelArray) library(rhdf5)  # Collect chunk HDF5 files and order by numeric chunk index chunk_files <- Sys.glob("result_chunk_*.h5") if (length(chunk_files) == 0) {   stop("No chunk HDF5 files found (expected files like result_chunk_1.h5).") } chunk_ids <- as.integer(sub("result_chunk_([0-9]+)\\.h5$", "\\1", basename(chunk_files))) if (anyNA(chunk_ids)) {   stop("Could not parse chunk index from one or more chunk filenames.") } chunk_files <- chunk_files[order(chunk_ids)]  # Read each chunk's saved results matrix + column names results_list <- lapply(chunk_files, function(fn) {   mat <- h5read(fn, "results/results_lm_chunk/results_matrix")   col_names <- h5read(fn, "results/results_lm_chunk/column_names")   df <- as.data.frame(mat)   colnames(df) <- as.character(col_names)   df })  # Optional consistency check across chunks if (!all(vapply(results_list[-1], function(x) identical(colnames(x), colnames(results_list[[1]])), logical(1)))) {   stop("Column names differ across chunk files; cannot concatenate safely.") }  full_result <- do.call(rbind, results_list)  # Verify we have all elements cat("Total rows:", nrow(full_result), "\n")  # Single-process write back to the main HDF5 file writeResults("data.h5", df.output = full_result, analysis_name = "results_lm")

text: ModelArray performs mass-univariate statistical analysis - fitting model independently every spatial location within brain mask. call spatial location element. Depending imaging modality, element one : Despite differences represent, ModelArray treats uniformly. analysis functions - ModelArray.lm(), ModelArray.gam(), ModelArray.wrap() - work regardless whether elements fixels, voxels, greyordinates.

what: Working with a subset of elements
title: Elements: The Unit of Analysis
text: element.subset parameter, available ModelArray.lm(), ModelArray.gam(), ModelArray.wrap(), lets run models subset elements rather . useful : Testing: run first 100 elements verify model works committing full run Batch processing: split large analysis across multiple HPC jobs (see vignette("element-splitting")) element.subset takes vector 1-based indices scalar matrix. example, element.subset = 1:100 runs elements IDs 0 99 (first 100 rows matrix).

title: Elements: The Unit of Analysis
text: voxel-wise vertexwise data, subjects may different brain coverage (subject-specific masks). means elements, particularly near edge analysis mask, may valid data subjects. ModelArray handles two threshold parameters ModelArray.lm() ModelArray.gam(): num.subj.lthr.abs: minimum number subjects required (absolute count) num.subj.lthr.rel: minimum proportion total subjects required (0–1) voxel analyzed number subjects valid (non-NaN) values exceeds thresholds. Otherwise, voxel skipped outputs set NaN. fixel-wise surface-based data, subjects typically share mask, thresholds needed.
code: # Require at least 10 subjects AND at least 20% of total subjects result <- ModelArray.lm(FA ~ Age + sex, modelarray, phenotypes, "FA",   num.subj.lthr.abs = 10,   num.subj.lthr.rel = 0.2 )

title: Understanding the HDF5 File
text: HDF5 (Hierarchical Data Format version 5) file format designed storing large, structured datasets. Think filesystem within file: Groups act like directories, organizing data hierarchically Datasets act like files, holding arrays data Attributes act like metadata, attached groups datasets HDF5 files use .h5 extension widely used scientific computing support -disk access (reading data without loading entire file memory), compression, efficient chunked storage.

title: Understanding the HDF5 File
text: ModelArray expects specific structure inside HDF5 file. structure created companion tools — ModelArrayIO fixel data, voxel data, CIFTI data. layout follows pattern:
code: / ├── scalars/ │   └── <scalar_name>/          # e.g., "FDC", "FA", "thickness" │       └── values              # Dataset: elements × source files matrix │       └── column_names        # Dataset (text): source filenames └── results/                    # Created by writeResults()     └── <analysis_name>/        # e.g., "results_lm", "results_gam"         ├── results_matrix      # Dataset: elements × statistics matrix         └── column_names        # Dataset (text): statistic column names

title: Understanding the HDF5 File
text: /scalars/<scalar_name>/values main data matrix: Rows = elements (fixels, voxels, greyordinates) Columns = source files /scalars/<scalar_name>/column_names stores source filenames correspond column, can trace every column back subject. modern ModelArrayIO, column names written dedicated text dataset long name vectors can exceed practical HDF5 attribute limits.

title: Understanding the HDF5 File
text: /results/<analysis_name>/results_matrix created call writeResults() save statistical outputs. analysis gets subgroup, can store multiple analyses (e.g., results_lm results_gam) file. corresponding statistic names stored /results/<analysis_name>/column_names text dataset.

title: Understanding the HDF5 File
text: Preferred (current): text dataset column_names Legacy (older files): attributes values/results_matrix (example column_names colnames) present, prefer column_names dataset canonical source treat attributes backward-compatibility metadata.

title: Understanding the HDF5 File
text: create ModelArray object, scalar data loaded memory. Instead, ModelArray uses DelayedArray framework create lazy reference -disk data: DelayedMatrix holds pointer HDF5 file. Data read disk actually access specific rows columns. ModelArray can handle datasets hundreds thousands elements thousands subjects without running memory. model fitting, ModelArray reads one element (row) time — pulling single row ~N subject values, fitting model, moving . point full matrix loaded RAM.
code: library(ModelArray)  modelarray <- ModelArray("data.h5", scalar_types = c("FDC")) scalars(modelarray)[["FDC"]] <602229 x 100> matrix of class DelayedMatrix and type "double":

title: Understanding the HDF5 File
text: can explore structure HDF5 file using rhdf5::h5ls(): can also read specific pieces data directly:
code: rhdf5::h5ls("data.h5") group             name       otype  dclass         dim 0                / analysis_configs   H5I_GROUP 1 /analysis_configs       results_lm   H5I_GROUP 2                /          results   H5I_GROUP 3         /results       results_lm   H5I_GROUP 4  /results/results_lm results_matrix H5I_DATASET   FLOAT 602229 x 17 5                /          scalars   H5I_GROUP 6         /scalars              FDC   H5I_GROUP 7      /scalars/FDC           values H5I_DATASET   FLOAT 602229 x 100 # Preferred: read names from the text dataset rhdf5::h5read("data.h5", "scalars/FDC/column_names")  # Legacy fallback for older files rhdf5::h5readAttributes("data.h5", "scalars/FDC/values")$column_names  # Read a small slice of the data matrix rhdf5::h5read("data.h5", "scalars/FDC/values", index = list(1:5, 1:3))

title: Understanding the HDF5 File
text: ModelArray create HDF5 files raw imaging data — ’s job companion conversion tools: command reads source imaging files listed cohort CSV writes HDF5 layout described . See ModelArrayIO documentation detailed usage instructions.

title: Using HDF5 for Large-Scale Analyses
text: typical fixel-based analysis might involve 600,000 fixels across 1,000 subjects. Stored naively double-precision matrix, ’s 4.5 GB — large hold memory systems, let alone alongside R session overhead model fitting. HDF5 solves keeping data disk providing efficient random access slices data. ModelArray leverages fit models element--element without ever loading full matrix RAM.

title: Using HDF5 for Large-Scale Analyses
text: HDF5 files store data chunks — rectangular blocks array read written unit. access single row (element), HDF5 reads chunk(s) contain row, entire dataset. chunk layout matters performance. ModelArray reads data row row (one element time), chunks span modest number rows columns work best. default behavior ModelArrayIO conversion commands (confixel, convoxel, concifti). creating HDF5 files confixel command ModelArrayIO, can control chunk size --chunkmb flag: Smaller chunks use less memory per read may require disk seeks. default 4 MB reasonable trade-datasets.
code: $ confixel \     --index-file FDC/index.mif \     --directions-file FDC/directions.mif \     --cohort-file cohort.csv \     --relative-root /path/to/project \     --output-hdf5 data.h5 \     --chunkmb 4       # chunk size in MB (default)

title: Using HDF5 for Large-Scale Analyses
text: HDF5 supports transparent compression chunks using gzip. enabled, data compressed disk decompressed --fly read. typically reduces file sizes 30–60% minimal impact read speed. ModelArrayIO enables gzip compression default. can see reflected output filenames: compression transparent ModelArray — reads data way regardless whether ’s compressed.
code: study-HBN_compression-gzip_chunkmb-4_thickness.h5

title: Using HDF5 for Large-Scale Analyses
text: call ModelArray.lm(), ModelArray.gam(), ModelArray.wrap(), happens element: Read one row scalar matrix (values subjects element) Combine phenotypes data frame create per-element data frame Fit model (lm, gam, custom function) Extract statistics single row output data frame Discard per-element data move next element point one row scalar matrix memory. ModelArray can handle arbitrarily large datasets — memory footprint determined number subjects (columns), number elements (rows).

title: Using HDF5 for Large-Scale Analyses
text: ModelArray supports parallel processing via n_cores parameter: uses parallel::mclapply() (fork-based parallelism Linux/macOS) process multiple elements simultaneously. forked worker inherits file handle reads different rows HDF5 file. HDF5 supports concurrent reads file, works safely scales well.
code: result <- ModelArray.lm(FDC ~ Age + sex, modelarray, phenotypes, "FDC",   n_cores = 4 )

title: Using HDF5 for Large-Scale Analyses
text: multiple processes can safely read HDF5 file, safely write simultaneously. standard HDF5 library (without special MPI-IO compilation) support concurrent writes — can corrupt file. means never multiple R processes calling writeResults() HDF5 file time. split analysis across HPC jobs (see vignette("element-splitting")), job save partial results separate .rds file, single final step combines writes HDF5 file.
code: # WRONG: multiple jobs writing to the same H5 simultaneously # writeResults("data.h5", df.output = my_partial_result, ...)  # Don't do this in parallel!  # RIGHT: save partial results, then combine in one process saveRDS(my_partial_result, sprintf("result_chunk_%d.rds", job_id))  # ... after all jobs finish, in a single process: chunks <- lapply(Sys.glob("result_chunk_*.rds"), readRDS) full_result <- do.call(rbind, chunks) writeResults("data.h5", df.output = full_result, analysis_name = "results_lm")

title: Using HDF5 for Large-Scale Analyses
text: writeResults() modifies HDF5 file place, ’s good practice back file writing results: something goes wrong write (e.g., process killed mid-write), HDF5 file may left inconsistent state. backup lets recover without re-running conversion step.
code: $ cp data.h5 data_backup.h5

text: GAMs useful expect nonlinear relationships - example, nonlinear changes brain structure across lifespan.

title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
text: Use s() specify smooth terms. k parameter sets upper limit degrees freedom smooth function: ModelArray prints summary smooth term settings run :
code: formula.gam <- FDC ~ s(Age, k = 4) + sex + dti64MeanRelRMS  result <- ModelArray.gam(formula.gam, modelarray, phenotypes, "FDC",   element.subset = 1:100,   method = "REML" ) The formula requested: FDC ~ s(Age, k = 4) + sex + dti64MeanRelRMS s(Age):   k = 4;   fx = FALSE (default);   bs = tp (default) method = REML (default: GCV.Cp)

title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
text: GAM output differs lm smooth terms vs parametric terms: Smooth terms (e.g., s(Age)): Parametric terms (e.g., sex, dti64MeanRelRMS): Model-level:

title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
text: Smooth terms parametric terms separate correction arguments:
code: result <- ModelArray.gam(formula.gam, modelarray, phenotypes, "FDC",   element.subset = 1:100,   correct.p.value.smoothTerms = c("fdr", "bonferroni"),   correct.p.value.parametricTerms = c("fdr", "bonferroni"),   method = "REML" )

title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
text: ModelArray supports full range mgcv::gam() smooth terms: s(): penalized regression splines (default thin-plate, bs = "tp") te(), ti(), t2(): tensor product smooths interactions continuous variables fx parameter controls whether smooth penalized (fx = FALSE, default) unpenalized/fixed (fx = TRUE). bs parameter selects basis type.

title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
text: ModelArray provides two helper functions common GAM formula patterns: Factor-smooth interaction (gen_gamFormula_fxSmooth): Generates y ~ orderedFactor + s(x) + s(x, =orderedFactor): Continuous interaction (gen_gamFormula_contIx): Generates y ~ ti(x) + ti(z) + ti(x, z):
code: result <- gen_gamFormula_fxSmooth(   response.var = "FDC",   factor.var = "group",   smooth.var = "Age",   phenotypes = phenotypes,   reference.group = "control",   fx = TRUE, k = 4 ) formula <- result$formula phenotypes <- result$phenotypes # may contain new ordered factor column formula <- gen_gamFormula_contIx(   response.var = "FDC",   cont1.var = "Age",   cont2.var = "BMI",   fx = TRUE, k = 4 )

title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
text: ModelArray.wrap() lets run R function across elements, using ModelArray’s efficient looping parallelization machinery.

title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
text: writing function, use exampleElementData() see per-element data frame looks like: returns data frame phenotype columns plus new column scalar value element. can explore like data frame:
code: # Using the HBN cortical thickness data h5_path <- "path/to/thickness.h5" modelarray <- ModelArray(h5_path, scalar_types = c("thickness")) phenotypes <- read.csv("path/to/phenotypes.csv")  example_df <- exampleElementData(modelarray,   scalar = "thickness", i_element = 12345, phenotypes = phenotypes ) names(example_df) library(ggplot2) ggplot(example_df, aes(x = age, y = thickness, color = study_site)) +   geom_point() +   stat_smooth(method = "lm") +   theme_minimal()

title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
text: function must accept single argument named data return single-row data frame (tibble):
code: my_site_analysis <- function(data) {   # Compute mean thickness per site   site_means <- data %>%     dplyr::group_by(study_site) %>%     dplyr::summarize(mean_thickness = mean(thickness, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%     tidyr::pivot_wider(       names_from = study_site,       values_from = mean_thickness,       names_glue = "thickness_{study_site}"     )    # Run ANOVA on site   site_anova <- lm(thickness ~ study_site, data = data) %>%     anova() %>%     broom::tidy() %>%     dplyr::filter(term != "Residuals") %>%     dplyr::select(term, statistic, p.value) %>%     tidyr::pivot_wider(       names_from = term,       values_from = c(statistic, p.value),       names_glue = "{term}.{.value}"     )    dplyr::bind_cols(site_means, site_anova) }

title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
text: Use analyseOneElement.wrap() verify function works ModelArray’s machinery:
code: analyseOneElement.wrap(   12345, my_site_analysis, modelarray, phenotypes, "thickness",   num.subj.lthr = 10, flag_initiate = TRUE, on_error = "debug" )

title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
code: result <- ModelArray.wrap(   FUN = my_site_analysis,   data = modelarray,   phenotypes = phenotypes,   scalar = "thickness",   n_cores = 8 )  head(result) writeResults(h5_path, df.output = result, analysis_name = "site_analysis")

what: Case study: harmonize with covfam and stream to a new scalar
title: Modelling in Depth: lm, gam, and wrap
text: can also use ModelArray.wrap() transformation engine rather test runner. example applies harmonization step per element, streams harmonized values directly /scalars, loads harmonized scalar new ModelArray. want stream model statistics (transformed scalars), use write_results_name write_results_file ModelArray.lm(), ModelArray.gam(), ModelArray.wrap().
code: library(covfam)  # Assume phenotypes has harmonization variables, e.g.: #   source_file, site, age, sex # and that "thickness" is already present in the h5 as an input scalar.  h5_in <- "thickness_raw.h5" h5_harmonized <- "thickness_harmonized.h5"  ma_raw <- ModelArray(h5_in, scalar_types = "thickness") phenotypes <- read.csv("phenotypes.csv")  # Copy metadata/layout into a new file so we can append new scalars there. file.copy(h5_in, h5_harmonized, overwrite = TRUE)  covfam_harmonize_element <- function(data) {   # Harmonize one element across subjects.   # Adjust arguments here to match your covfam configuration.   out <- covfam::covfam(     y = data$thickness,     batch = data$site,     mod = data.frame(age = data$age, sex = data$sex)   )    # Return named subject-level vector so wrap columns map to source_file order.   vals <- out$y_harmonized   names(vals) <- data$source_file   vals }  # Stream harmonized subject-level values into scalars/thickness_covfam/values. # Set return_output = FALSE to avoid keeping the full output table in memory. ModelArray.wrap(   FUN = covfam_harmonize_element,   data = ma_raw,   phenotypes = phenotypes,   scalar = "thickness",   n_cores = 8,   write_scalar_name = "thickness_covfam",   write_scalar_file = h5_harmonized,   write_scalar_flush_every = 2000L,   return_output = FALSE )  # Load the harmonized scalar as a new modelling input ma_harmonized <- ModelArray(h5_harmonized, scalar_types = c("thickness_covfam"))  # Use harmonized data in downstream models fit_harmonized <- ModelArray.lm(   thickness_covfam ~ age + sex,   data = ma_harmonized,   phenotypes = phenotypes,   scalar = "thickness_covfam",   n_cores = 8 )

title: An R package for statistical analysis of fixel-wise data and beyond
text: Mass-univariate statistical modeling large neuroimaging datasets without supercomputer Brain images contain hundreds thousands spatial locations. Fitting statistical model every single one - fixel, voxel, cortical vertex - conceptually straightforward practically painful: data large load memory, code needed loop efficiently across elements tedious write. ModelArray handles . write one model formula ModelArray fits across every element dataset, parallel, reading needs disk moment. result tidy data frame statistics - one row per element - ready thresholding visualization. ’s . results data frame estimates, t-statistics, p-values, FDR-corrected p-values every fixel.
code: library(ModelArray)  modelarray <- ModelArray("data.h5", scalar_types = c("FDC")) phenotypes  <- read.csv("cohort.csv")  results <- ModelArray.lm(FDC ~ Age + sex + motion, modelarray, phenotypes,   n_cores = 4 )

title: An R package for statistical analysis of fixel-wise data and beyond
text: scales. ModelArray uses HDF5 -disk storage DelayedArray lazy access. ABCC dMRI dataset ~350,000 voxels × ~26,000-sessions - 291 GB loaded entirety - never enters RAM. ModelArray reads one element time, memory usage stays flat regardless dataset size. ’s flexible. Beyond linear models, ModelArray supports: GAMs (ModelArray.gam()) penalized splines capturing nonlinear effects, lifespan trajectories accelerate decelerate across development R modeling function (ModelArray.wrap()) — can write function takes data frame returns row statistics, ModelArray run across entire dataset works . ModelArray modality-agnostic — code works fixel-wise data, voxel-wise data, surface-based greyordinate data. companion tool ModelArrayIO handles conversion .mif, NIfTI, CIFTI formats HDF5 file ModelArray expects. ’s peer-reviewed. ModelArray published NeuroImage 2023 used studies lifespan brain development.

Must one names(scalars(data)). element.subset Integer vector element indices (1-based) run. Default NULL, .e. elements data. full.outputs Logical. TRUE, return full set statistics (ignoring var.* arguments). FALSE (default), return requested var.* correct.p.value.*. var.smoothTerms Character vector. Statistics save smooth terms, broom::tidy(parametric = FALSE). See Details. var.parametricTerms Character vector. Statistics save parametric terms, broom::tidy(parametric = TRUE). title: Example per-element data.frame for user functions — exampleElementData
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ h5_path <- system.file("extdata", "n50_fixels.h5", package = "ModelArray") csv_path <- system.file("extdata", "n50_cohort.csv", package = "ModelArray") ma <- ModelArray(h5_path, scalar_types = c("FD")) phen <- read.csv(csv_path) df <- exampleElementData(ma, scalar = "FD", i_element = 1, phenotypes = phen)  # Now test your custom function on this single element: my_fun <- function(data, ...) {   mod <- lm(FD ~ age + sex, data = data)   broom::tidy(mod) } my_fun(data = df) } # }

title: Generate GAM formula with continuous-by-continuous interaction — gen_gamFormula_contIx

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ formula <- gen_gamFormula_contIx(   response.var = "FD",   cont1.var = "age",   cont2.var = "cognition" ) formula  # Use in ModelArray.gam with changed R-squared for the interaction results <- ModelArray.gam(   formula,   data = ma,   phenotypes = phenotypes,   scalar = "FD",   changed.rsq.term.index = list(3) ) } # }

text: response.var Character. variable name response (dependent variable), typically scalar name like "FD". factor.var Character. variable name factor. ordered factor phenotypes. , ordered factor generated new column, requires reference.group. smooth.var Character. variable name smooth term main effect (e.g. "age"). phenotypes data.frame cohort columns independent variables, including factor.var smooth.var. reference.group Character. reference (baseline) group ordered factor factor.var. Required factor.var phenotypes already ordered factor. prefix.ordered.factor Character. Prefix ordered factor column name. Required factor.var phenotypes already ordered factor. Default "o". fx Logical. Passed s. TRUE (recommended), smooth treated fixed degrees freedom. Default TRUE. k Integer NULL. Basis dimension passed s main smooth interaction terms. NULL (default), uses default mgcv::s().

title: Generate GAM formula with factor-smooth interaction — gen_gamFormula_fxSmooth
text: list two components: formula generated formula object. phenotypes (possibly updated) data.frame. factor.var already ordered factor, new column named paste0(prefix.ordered.factor, factor.var) added. Otherwise identical input.

title: Generate GAM formula with factor-smooth interaction — gen_gamFormula_fxSmooth
text: helper exists setting factor-smooth interactions gam requires ordered factor specific formula structure. factor.var phenotypes already ordered factor, function creates one using reference.group baseline level adds new column (named prefix.ordered.factor prepended factor.var). returned phenotypes data.frame must used subsequent ModelArray.gam call ordered factor column available model.

title: Generate GAM formula with factor-smooth interaction — gen_gamFormula_fxSmooth
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ phenotypes <- read.csv("cohort.csv")  # factor.var is not yet ordered - function creates it result <- gen_gamFormula_fxSmooth(   response.var = "FD",   factor.var = "sex",   smooth.var = "age",   phenotypes = phenotypes,   reference.group = "female" ) result$formula  # Use the updated phenotypes (contains the ordered factor column) results <- ModelArray.gam(   result$formula,   data = ma,   phenotypes = result$phenotypes,   scalar = "FD" ) } # }

text: modelarrays list least two ModelArray objects, constructed different HDF5 file. phenotypes_list list data.frames, one per ModelArray modelarrays. must contain source_file column whose entries match corresponding ModelArray's sources (.e. sources(modelarrays[[]])). must also contain columns named merge_on. merge_on Character vector column names present data.frames phenotypes_list, used inner-join subjects across sessions/modalities (e.g. c("subject_id")). combination columns must uniquely identify subject within data.frame.

title: Merge multiple ModelArrays from different HDF5 files — mergeModelArrays
text: list two components: data combined ModelArray containing scalars inputs. scalar's columns subsetted reordered match inner-joined subject list. phenotypes inner-joined data.frame. Original source_file columns renamed source_file.<scalar_name> new unified source_file column added use analysis functions.

title: Merge multiple ModelArrays from different HDF5 files — mergeModelArrays
text: merge performs inner join phenotype data.frames columns specified merge_on. subjects present phenotype data.frames retained. Scalar matrices input ModelArray column-subsetted reordered match joined subject list. unified source_file column created merge_on columns downstream analysis functions (ModelArray.lm, ModelArray.gam, ModelArray.wrap) can align phenotypes scalars. original source_file columns renamed source_file.<first_scalar_name> input ModelArray. Scalar names must unique across input ModelArrays. two ModelArrays share scalar name (e.g. "FD"), function error. Element counts (number rows) must match across scalars. element metadata available (see elementMetadata), function checks consistent across inputs warns differs partially available.

title: Merge multiple ModelArrays from different HDF5 files — mergeModelArrays
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Load two sessions from different h5 files ma1 <- ModelArray("session1.h5", scalar_types = c("FD")) ma2 <- ModelArray("session2.h5", scalar_types = c("FC")) phen1 <- read.csv("session1_cohort.csv") phen2 <- read.csv("session2_cohort.csv")  # Merge on subject ID merged <- mergeModelArrays(   modelarrays = list(ma1, ma2),   phenotypes_list = list(phen1, phen2),   merge_on = "subject_id" )  # Use the merged object for cross-scalar analysis merged$data scalarNames(merged$data) # c("FD", "FC") head(merged$phenotypes)  results <- ModelArray.lm(   FD ~ age + sex + FC,   data = merged$data,   phenotypes = merged$phenotypes,   scalar = c("FD", "FC") ) } # }
